Knowing when to use 'a' and when to use 'the' can be quite difficult, and as a native English speaker it's hard for me to remember the exact grammatical rules that may apply to this situation, but I will give as much advice as I can.Using 'a' is common when speaking of a single object, or one of many. "I read a newspaper." or "I read a NHK business English textbook." In the second example, it sounds as if there are many NHK business textbooks and you have read one of them. Even though the name is actually, "The Japan Times", you wouldn't say, "I read the The Japan Times." English just cuts one of those out. Since this probably didn't answer your question very well, I'll try to write some more examples to help you find a pattern for yourself. text: In other words,`My funny lover`?
correct: In other words, does it mean "My funny lover"? (Not necessarily. Words, according to context and the way in which they are spoken can easily change their meaning. Originally, the phrase meant something like "my friend Valentine is (a little) funny, but I like him". According to a Wikipedia page: "In the original play, a character named Billie Smith (played by Mitzi Green) sings the song to Valentine "Val" LaMar (played by Ray Heatherton). In the song, Billie pokes fun at some of Valentine's characteristics, but ultimately affirms that he [Valentine] makes her smile and that she doesn't want him to change." If you like wordplays: "a valentine" has also a different, more general (non-jazz related) meaning. From Wordnet http://wordnetweb.princeton.edu/perl/webwn?s=valentine [1. valentine (a sweetheart chosen to receive a greeting on Saint Valentine's Day) "will you be my valentine?" 2. Valentine (a card sent or given (as to a sweetheart) on Saint Valentine's Day)]. So to confirm your way of reading it, yes, you can easily imagine a situation when a playful woman (in the West, unlike in Japan it is usually a woman who receives a Valentine card or some Valentine's sweets) says to her friend "you're my funny Valentine" which could be translated to male speech as "I think you are funny, but you are sweet, so you are my piece of chocolate now." or "You're as funny and as sweet as a card for me on Saint Valentine's day.") Just a guess. Oh, and the Wiki page is at: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/My_Funny_Valentine

text: So, most Anime written in Japan can be sensed a stream of Justice.
correct: So in most Anime (written in Japan), there is a sense of justice that can be felt. . In the west, when we think of anime we always assume Japanese anime, because for many people anime is specific to Japan, where as in Japan anime (standing for "animation") usually means anything that is animated, including what we in the west call cartoons like Bugs Bunny etc. Therefore "written in Japan" is not really necessary, but it does emphasis it coming from Japan.

text: My elder brother, who learn programming, talked a lot with him.
correct: My elder brother, who learns programming, talked a lot with him. The singular noun [brother] requires a singular form of the verb [learn]. "Who learns? My brother learns that's who."

text: And everyone, thanks for reading this long entry, and have happy holidays!
correct: And everyone, thanks for reading this long entry, and have a happy holidays! (I was a little confused why we use a singular indefinite article for "happy holidays" instead of a plural. I guess we consider "holidays" as a collective noun, but I guess in order to avoid any nitpickings from grammar nazis, you can just say "Happy Holidays!" instead of "Have a Happy Holidays!")

text: Several years later, I had a baby and quited my job before delivering the baby.
correct: Several years later, I had a baby and quit my job before delivering the baby. (There is no such word as quited in English. Quit is already in the past. PRESENT TENSE: I am quitting my job. FUTURE TENSE: I will quit my job. PAST TENSE: I quit my job.)
